prompt.content:
Use file “Cards_Patient 10” to access the entire medical history / record for the patient. Please use the patient tab as the source of truth for all demographic information. Disregard patient identifiers (Name, Age, DOB, MRN) found within the text of the clinicalnotes tab as they have been shifted for de-identification purposes

-----

Patient 10 presents to the clinic for a visit with their primary care doctor. 

Based on the recent conversation, which new diagnosis should be ruled out for this patient? Which medication should be changed, if any? In addition, which additional tests should the patient undergo to rule out this new diagnosis?
Answers should align with the most current clinical guidelines as of January 10, 2026.

extra.task_name: Web Search_Attachments_Cards Patient 10 - Primary Aldosteronism - Amrinder K

golden_responses.golden_text_response:
Based on the recent conversation, which new diagnosis should be ruled out for this patient? Which medication should be changed? In addition, which additional tests should the patient undergo to rule out this new diagnosis? 

The clinician adheres to the 2025 American College of Cardiology and American Heart Association joint clinical practice guidelines for hypertension. They state that the patient has a diagnosis of primary hypertension and presents with muscle cramps. Laboratory results show normal electrolyte levels. Spontaneous hypokalemia occurs in only 20% to 50% of patients with primary aldosteronism. Therefore, primary aldosteronism should be ruled out in patients with primary hypertension who present with muscle cramps. In this case, primary aldosteronism is a new diagnosis to be considered and ruled out. There is no indication that a medication change is necessary at this time. Additionally, the clinician recommends that the plasma aldosterone/renin activity ratio be ordered.

3.2.3. Secondary Forms of Hypertension section on Page e 133 and 134.
https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/pdf/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001356
